1 Welcome

This tutorial introduces multiple regression and generalized linear models (GLMs) in R. These tools allow you to model relationships between a response variable and multiple predictors, test interactions, compare models using AIC, and analyze count and binary data.

By the end of this tutorial, you will be able to:

  • Fit multiple linear regression models
  • Interpret coefficients with multiple predictors
  • Specify and interpret interaction terms
  • Compare models using AIC
  • Fit GLMs for count (Poisson) and binary (logistic) data

2 Setup

# Load required packages
library(tidyverse)

3 Part 1: Multiple Linear Regression

3.1 Example Data

We will use the built-in mtcars dataset.

data(mtcars)
head(mtcars)
##                    mpg cyl disp  hp drat    wt  qsec vs am gear carb
## Mazda RX4         21.0   6  160 110 3.90 2.620 16.46  0  1    4    4
## Mazda RX4 Wag     21.0   6  160 110 3.90 2.875 17.02  0  1    4    4
## Datsun 710        22.8   4  108  93 3.85 2.320 18.61  1  1    4    1
## Hornet 4 Drive    21.4   6  258 110 3.08 3.215 19.44  1  0    3    1
## Hornet Sportabout 18.7   8  360 175 3.15 3.440 17.02  0  0    3    2
## Valiant           18.1   6  225 105 2.76 3.460 20.22  1  0    3    1

Suppose we want to predict fuel efficiency (mpg) using:

  • Car weight (wt)
  • Horsepower (hp)

3.2 Fitting a Multiple Regression Model

model1 <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars)
summary(model1)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars)
## 
## Residuals:
##    Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
## -3.941 -1.600 -0.182  1.050  5.854 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) 37.22727    1.59879  23.285  < 2e-16 ***
## wt          -3.87783    0.63273  -6.129 1.12e-06 ***
## hp          -0.03177    0.00903  -3.519  0.00145 ** 
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 2.593 on 29 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.8268, Adjusted R-squared:  0.8148 
## F-statistic: 69.21 on 2 and 29 DF,  p-value: 9.109e-12

3.2.1 Interpretation

  • Each coefficient represents the effect of a predictor holding the others constant
  • The intercept is the predicted mpg when all predictors are zero (often not meaningful)

3.3 Visualizing Partial Relationships

# Added-variable (partial regression) plot
library(car)
## Loading required package: carData
## 
## Attaching package: 'car'
## The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':
## 
##     recode
## The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':
## 
##     some
avPlots(model1)

4 Part 2: Interaction Terms

Sometimes the effect of one predictor depends on another.

Example: Does the effect of weight on mpg depend on horsepower?

model2 <- lm(mpg ~ wt * hp, data = mtcars)
summary(model2)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = mpg ~ wt * hp, data = mtcars)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -3.0632 -1.6491 -0.7362  1.4211  4.5513 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) 49.80842    3.60516  13.816 5.01e-14 ***
## wt          -8.21662    1.26971  -6.471 5.20e-07 ***
## hp          -0.12010    0.02470  -4.863 4.04e-05 ***
## wt:hp        0.02785    0.00742   3.753 0.000811 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 2.153 on 28 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.8848, Adjusted R-squared:  0.8724 
## F-statistic: 71.66 on 3 and 28 DF,  p-value: 2.981e-13

4.0.1 Reading an Interaction

  • wt:hp represents how the slope of wt changes with hp
  • Interaction models should usually include the main effects

4.1 Plotting an Interaction

mtcars %>%
  mutate(hp_group = if_else(hp > median(hp), "High HP", "Low HP")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(wt, mpg, color = hp_group)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

5 Part 3: Model Selection with AIC

AIC (Akaike Information Criterion) balances model fit and complexity.

Lower AIC is better.

model_simple <- lm(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars)
model_full   <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp + cyl, data = mtcars)

AIC(model_simple, model_full, model1, model2)
##              df      AIC
## model_simple  3 166.0294
## model_full    5 155.4766
## model1        4 156.6523
## model2        5 145.6109

5.0.1 Rules of Thumb

  • ΔAIC < 2 → models are similarly supported
  • Prefer simpler models when AICs are close

6 Part 4: Generalized Linear Models (GLMs)

Linear regression assumes:

  • Normally distributed errors
  • Constant variance

GLMs allow different distributions and link functions.

6.1 GLM for Count Data (Poisson Regression)

6.1.1 Example

Suppose we count events (e.g., number of species).

# Simulated count data
set.seed(42)
df_count <- tibble(
  x = runif(50, 0, 10),
  count = rpois(50, lambda = exp(1 + 0.3 * x))
)
poisson_model <- glm(count ~ x, family = poisson, data = df_count)
summary(poisson_model)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = count ~ x, family = poisson, data = df_count)
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)  0.89466    0.12032   7.436 1.04e-13 ***
## x            0.31329    0.01449  21.623  < 2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for poisson family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 685.241  on 49  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance:  37.871  on 48  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 270.93
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4

6.1.2 Interpretation

  • Coefficients are on the log scale
  • exp(coef) gives multiplicative effects
exp(coef(poisson_model))
## (Intercept)           x 
##    2.446504    1.367918

6.2 Checking Fit

# Plot observed vs predicted
df_count$predicted <- predict(poisson_model, type = "response")

ggplot(df_count, aes(x, count)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_line(aes(y = predicted), color = "blue")

7 Part 5: GLM for Binary Data (Logistic Regression)

7.0.1 Example

Binary outcomes (yes/no, presence/absence).

# Simulated binary data
set.seed(123)
df_binary <- tibble(
  x = rnorm(100),
  y = rbinom(100, 1, plogis(-0.5 + 1.2 * x))
)
logit_model <- glm(y ~ x, family = binomial, data = df_binary)
summary(logit_model)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = y ~ x, family = binomial, data = df_binary)
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)  -0.3097     0.2296  -1.349    0.177    
## x             1.2663     0.3080   4.111 3.94e-05 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 137.99  on 99  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 114.76  on 98  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 118.76
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4

7.0.2 Interpretation

  • Coefficients are in log-odds
  • exp(coef) gives odds ratios
exp(coef(logit_model))
## (Intercept)           x 
##   0.7337018   3.5478259

7.1 Predicted Probabilities

df_binary$prob <- predict(logit_model, type = "response")

ggplot(df_binary, aes(x, prob)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess")
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

8 Model Comparison for GLMs

AIC works for GLMs as well:

logit_null <- glm(y ~ 1, family = binomial, data = df_binary)
AIC(logit_null, logit_model)
##             df      AIC
## logit_null   1 139.9888
## logit_model  2 118.7598

9 Key Takeaways ✅

  • Multiple regression allows multiple predictors
  • Interaction terms change how predictors work together
  • AIC helps compare non-nested models
  • GLMs extend regression to non-normal data
  • Interpretation depends on the link function

10 Common Mistakes

  • ❌ Ignoring interactions when theoretically important
  • ❌ Using linear regression for count or binary data
  • ❌ Interpreting GLM coefficients on the raw scale
  • ❌ Overfitting complex models with little data

11 Congratulations 🎉

You now have the tools to fit and interpret multiple regression models and GLMs—a major step toward real-world data analysis in environmental and social sciences.
